rules.title: {source} corrupts {target}'s nightly information
rules.description: {source} can drunk or poison {target}, causing them to receive false information each night. The Storyteller may give {target} any result, actively misleading good players.

rules.title: {source} corrupts {target}'s conditional information
rules.description: {source} can poison or drunk {target} before their trigger condition fires, meaning the resulting information is false at the worst possible moment.

rules.title: {source} disables {target}'s protection
rules.description: {source} poisons or drunks {target}, causing their protection ability to malfunction. Players {target} tries to protect may still be killed as though unprotected.

rules.title: {source} can corrupt {target}'s opening information
rules.description: If {target} is drunk or poisoned on the first night, their starting information is false. {source} acting before or on night 1 denies good a reliable anchor.

rules.title: {source} may taint {target}'s death information
rules.description: If {target} is drunk or poisoned when they die, the information triggered by their death is false. {source} can time this to corrupt the reveal.

rules.title: {source} undermines {target}'s repeated information
rules.description: {source}'s presence warps the reliability of all information gathering. {target}'s nightly results cannot be fully trusted with {source} in play.

rules.title: {source} corrupts {target}'s death reveal
rules.description: {source} can cause {target}'s death-triggered information to be false — the most impactful moment to mislead good is precisely when they expect a reliable reveal.

rules.title: {source} taints {target}'s conditional triggers
rules.description: When {target}'s ability fires based on a condition, {source} ensures that result is unreliable. Players relying on this as confirmation will be misled.

rules.title: {source} taints {target}'s starting information
rules.description: With {source} in play, any first-night information from {target} may already be unreliable from the start of the game.

rules.title: {source} can bypass {target}'s protection
rules.description: {source} adds an extra kill source that may not be blocked by {target}'s protection mechanic. This erodes the safety net {target} provides.

rules.title: {source} can kill {target} before their power fires
rules.description: {source}'s extra kill can eliminate {target} before they use their once-per-game ability, permanently wasting good's resource.

rules.title: {source} and {target} stack additional kill pressure
rules.description: Multiple evil kill sources mean good can lose players faster than expected. With both {source} and {target} in play, more than one person may die per night.

rules.title: {source} can threaten {target} outside normal execution
rules.description: {source} adds a kill source on good's side, threatening {target} independently of the day vote and creating pressure evil must account for.

rules.title: {source} and {target} layer protection for good
rules.description: Multiple protection abilities make it harder for evil to remove key good players. Stacked protection slows evil's night kill efficiency.

rules.title: {source} and {target} together extend evil's longevity
rules.description: Stacking demon-resilience mechanics makes the demon harder to find and kill. Good needs to account for extended game length and more night kills.

rules.title: {source} and {target} make evil difficult to eliminate on either front
rules.description: {source} keeps the demon in play longer while {target} guards evil players from execution. Combined, good struggles to reduce evil numbers efficiently.

rules.title: {source} may produce a false trigger for {target}
rules.description: If {source} fakes death or survives in an unexpected way, {target}'s death-triggered ability may fire on misleading information or not fire at all when expected.

rules.title: {source} and {target} flood good with cross-referenceable data
rules.description: Multiple recurring information sources let good players cross-check results each night, dramatically shrinking the space of possible evil configurations.

rules.title: {source} and {target} give good a strong opening picture
rules.description: Multiple first-night information roles let good triangulate character positions early. Cross-referencing these starting clues quickly narrows the evil candidates.

rules.title: {source} anchors the early game for {target} to build on
rules.description: {source}'s opening information gives {target} a baseline to cross-reference each night, turning a single data point into a running verification chain.

rules.title: {source} and {target} give good information at multiple stages
rules.description: {target} gathers information continuously while {source} provides a burst of data when death occurs. Together they cover both the ongoing and crisis information needs.

rules.title: {source}'s kills can trigger {target}'s death information
rules.description: When {source} kills outside the normal execution, {target}'s death-triggered ability may fire on that player, giving good bonus information from the unexpected death.

rules.title: {source} and {target} create high-stakes execution tension
rules.description: Executions become critical decision points when both {source} and {target} are in play. Forcing or preventing an execution has outsized consequences.

rules.title: {source}'s trap may be disrupted by {target}'s poison
rules.description: If {source}'s ability depends on being sober and healthy, {target} can drunk or poison them to prevent the trap from triggering correctly on the pivotal execution.

rules.title: {source} and {target} compound execution-phase chaos
rules.description: {source} creates stakes around executions while {target} muddies the information good uses to choose who to execute. Together they make nominations treacherous.

rules.title: {source} invalidates {target}'s information over time
rules.description: As alignments shift due to {source}, past information from {target} becomes unreliable retroactively. Players may be tracking an evil player who is now good, or vice versa.

rules.title: {source} and {target} layer alignment unpredictability
rules.description: Multiple alignment-shifting mechanics in play means team loyalty is fluid throughout the game. Good cannot fully trust their read on anyone when two such forces are active.

rules.title: {source} and {target} multiply character-swap chaos
rules.description: Two character-changing abilities in play means the pool of active roles is unpredictable. Information gathered about specific characters quickly becomes stale.

rules.title: {source} may invalidate {target}'s first-night information
rules.description: If characters change after night 1, information {target} received about specific character types may no longer be accurate as the pool has shifted.

rules.title: {source} alters the pool that {target} reads
rules.description: {source} changes which character types are in play before the game begins, affecting what {target} will confirm or deny on the first night.

rules.title: {source} and {target} compound setup complexity
rules.description: Multiple setup-modifying abilities mean character counts and the starting game state are significantly altered, increasing confusion about the composition.

rules.title: {source} and {target} overlap with layered madness mechanics
rules.description: Two madness-imposing abilities create complex ST demands and conflicting pressures on players. Managing both simultaneously is significantly harder for the Storyteller.

rules.title: {source} and {target} stack information corruption
rules.description: With both {source} and {target} in play, good players face layered unreliability: active poisoning plus passive information warping make clean deduction very difficult.

rules.title: {source} stays useful longer when shielded by {target}
rules.description: {target} keeps {source} alive through the night, maximizing the number of information results good can gather over the game.

rules.title: {target} ensures {source} survives to use their ability
rules.description: {target} protects {source} from night kills, reducing the risk of {source} dying before their once-per-game power can be activated.
